speaker: interviewer
text: --in 1996 in Norwalk, Connecticut. Language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the attack of the American fighter bombers. Could you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train was pulling out, and we were just in the open country when two American fighter bombers attacked the train. The two engines got shot out, and a number of SS and some prisoners got killed. Then the German guards jumped right off the train and surrounded the whole train so that nobody should escape. And then we were sitting there for-- the Americans dropped some bombs on the town. The town was called [? Reitzenheim. ?] And we tried to get away from this,and the population came right away and were very hostile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sudeten Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They surrounded the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the SS. Some Sudeten Germans came right away out. And we were-- had to pick up a few SS men to take him to a-- a hospital, who got wounded. When he came back, the Germans were separating the healthy ones from the sick ones already. We had to line up. And then a car with six men came on with machine pistols. And they start to open fire right away at the-- at the sick ones. Then they picked out maybe a dozen fellows and told them, you stay here. You throw the bodies in the box car. And then I was marched off towards Czechoslovakia. We were marched in groups of 200. And there were maybe 25 SS men in the-- guarding the 200. 10 on each side and five in the back. And after a while, we heard some shooting. Anyone who fell behind or could not keep up got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into Czechoslovakia. We were told that. And in the first night, we were in a factory. And there was a house nearby, and some fellows stole a few potatoes because we had nothing to eat. And the woman caught one of them, and she reported it to an SS man, and the fellow was shot right away. And then we marched for a couple of days. And wherever we went through the village which were German, the [GERMAN] was the German old people standing there with rifle and watching, making sure that nobody could escape. When we went to districts which were Czech, we found bread and things on the road. We could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We could pick up. Then on-- I think on the third or fourth day, we arrived in Leitmeritz, in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a small camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A small camp, mostly for Czechs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to 500. We left over 1,500 in-- in [GERMAN] when we left. So a few escaped, which I found out later on. But I would say 1,000 got-- at least 1,000 got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you housed once you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed overnight in the camp on the camp ground. Next morning, we were called up for roll call, and all Jews step out. So we had no other choice, step over to the side. And then we were marched to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what Theresienstadt looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was a-- a small village which was originally [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] built by Maria Theresa as a fortress in the-- in the-- in the 1800s. And it was a-- a garrison for the Czech army. And the Germans took everyone out and made it a concentration camp. Now, there were a few camps in Theresienstadt, the big camp and the-- the-- and the little camp. The old fortress--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in the old fortress. And when we arrived, we were put right away in isolation, not to have contact with anyone, and they gave us a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other Jews, yes. Only Jews were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that day, none. And then later on in the afternoon, we got marched to one of the barracks which was called the [GERMAN]. And we were put with 25 in one room to sleep on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the name of the barracks mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hamburg is a city. [GERMAN] means barrack of Hamburg. Barrack of [GERMAN]. Barrack of [GERMAN]. You know, so far. That's how they were named. Who named them, I don't know. Because Theresienstadt was a-- a ghetto, which I heard later on, that over 80,000 Jews from Theresienstadt were sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we marched with the-- to the barracks, people were right or left and asked, did you see that person? Did you see this person? Did you see this person? Where did you come from? They were-- we-- we asked what is the person-- when does the person leave here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, well, they all went to Auschwitz. And we started laughing because nobody in Theresienstadt knew what Auschwitz was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't tell them anything the first day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the second day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no contact with except for few Czechs who run the barracks. And they were no angels.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czechs who ran the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who-- who were in charge there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe their treatment of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were stealing food from us. We found that out afterwards. And then we had a number of death on the first night, on the-- bodies were swarming the hallways. Some even throw the bloody folk the balcony of the third floor right to the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the bodies removed the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bodies were-- I don't know they were moved the next day or not. I don't know. Anyway, they threw them in-- in one of the build-- down behind one of the buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they were buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because a few days later, I started to work at the crematoria. That's how I knew there was nobody buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me as-- did you hear noises around you as the war ended? Did you know that something was going on outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we knew the war would go to end because we had-- we knew the Americans were coming in the middle of Germany already when we-- when they moved us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Theresienstadt, we were isolated. And the people in Theresienstadt knew nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no news?

speaker: interviewee
text: --comparison. Nothing there. And then we were kept isolated in those barracks. And a few days later, they came along and they wanted some people to work. You would get double rations, they said.

speaker: interviewee
text: So another fellow I was together with in Jungfernhof said, hey, let's go. What can be so bad? Here we look at four walls and if they're not give us anything to eat, maybe we get something to eat. Yeah. But what we did was unloading bodies from the box cars. Where were the box cars coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the outside. Prisoners the Germans were-- dead prisoners or some were alive, they were shipping in. On the-- were 50 to 60 in a box car, up to 40 box cars at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel as you did that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since I had handled bodies in Theresienstadt, so it didn't bother me. The only argument I had was-- was with a doctor. He said, I have to look at the bodies first before you can take them out. Only it took him too long. And I said to him, look. They smell already. Anything move, you can have. What doesn't move is go on the wagon, because we are not sitting here all night waiting for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do this for?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did that every day till-- for a week, maybe. Then they took me to work in a crematoria on and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till July 11. I was-- I-- when the Russians took-- came in Theresienstadt, those who worked in the crematoria, also some others who were in charge, they all run away. They were Czechs. And so I got stuck with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that this was after liberation? Did you know this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Russians said, you are free. But 24 hours later, they arrested us and they-- we had to go back to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, you are volunteers from now on. Because you have typhus fever in the camp, and if you cannot control it you all go through the smokestack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the charge that they arrested you on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You have to go back to work. You are important person here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they forced you to work at the crematoriums?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was an outbreak of typhus? So you worked April, May, June, July?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 11. What happened then July 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the epidemic was down. There were very few who died on-- Germans prisoners were doing the work because I was supervising them already for quite a while. So they said I can go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They two-- gave me two choices. I could stay in Czechoslovakia for the service I had rendered with the typhus epidemic, or I could go to Germany. But they said, there's nothing in Germany. I'm better off to stay here. But since the Russians had so well-behaved and so much trouble was in Theresienstadt, the combination, especially by Russian citizens. Because were some things. The Latvian Jews blamed the German Jews for the massacres of the Germans-- of the Latvians in 1941 because they killed the Latvians to make room for the Germans. Some Latvian Jews-- who spoke Russian, said we are Russian citizen-- were accusing German Jews of collaboration. The Russians arrested some, and some had to run. And also I saw quite a few things which never should have happened. The delousing process was going too slow, even though there was more clothing than they even could handle. But nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation. Till the Americans shipped in DDT. Then they got rid of the lice. Not before. Thousands died which had-- did not have to die if they would have put a little [INAUDIBLE] behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made-- what method of transportation did you have to use to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Railroad trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you pay for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. I had a repatriation slip which gave me free ride by the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the slip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czech government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find as you approached your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the hometown-- the church was bombed and nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were your thoughts as you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought-- I-- I hope all those Nazis are around so I can knock a few teeth out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are-- were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: 23. What did you find when you saw your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: First-- well, I-- I was not feeling well. And I could not walk any stairs. So I told-- I met a friend. We said-- he said, stay with me. I said, no. I'm going to hospital. Something is wrong with me. And I went to the hospital, and I had a blood pressure under 90.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the hospital in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I had gained too much weight--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after the liberation and retained the body fluid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was up to over 180 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you find from your family in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. There were no Jews left. And I found out when they got deported and where and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did everyone end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Riga. With Riga. The rest went up to Theresienstadt. Because they were only old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father got liberated. He was in-- worked for the army. The [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means army clothing office 701. And he got shipped from Riga to Libau, and from Libau to Hamburg. He was there in the central prison. From there to the concentration camp in [GERMAN]. And then Count Bernadotte came into the act with him now. And he got turned over on May 1 to the Swedish government. And they were sent to Sweden with the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest of your family was sent with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you reunited with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two-- not until two years after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I found out that they were alive towards the end of 1945. Because somebody got a newspaper from England. The names were listed there, who went to Sweden. Because there was no mail you can send outside of Germany or coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the hospital for six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: For six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the hospital, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I throw the Nazi out of the house, which he had owned, and I took an apartment in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your own house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you reclaim your family house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not reclaim it in the beginning. Later on, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What made you hear about Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew there were trials going on, but it was very difficult to travel from one zone to the other. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What zone were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: British. And the British were a little bit anti-Jewish also, sorry to say. Because a little trouble was going on in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So you saw a British newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend-- a friend saw a British newspaper where the name of my family was in. But there was a-- the newspaper were printing about the Nuremberg Trials, which was on the censorship of the military government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had gone to a-- how did the circumstances come that you heard specifically about the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was this way. My brother married to a girl from Lodz in Sweden in 1947. Early '47. And he wrote me that-- then the mail was going-- that his brother-in-law is in Furth. That's next to Nuremberg. If I could visit him and see what he's doing. And I wrote back, yeah. I will go there. And I contact this fellow that I would go there. And then we had a meeting of the survivors in Dusseldorf. When I went to the meeting, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were all talking about going to Palestine. It was the last thing I wanted to do. So I left the meeting and I visited a cousin of my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here was a-- a girl in uniform. First, he wouldn't let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when finally he talked to her, and I-- and I said, I have to go to Furth next week. We were talking about the cousin. She said, I'm stationed in Nuremberg. And then as joke, I said, hey, that we can go out. Have a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she answered, but-- yeah. But don't have any ideas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she made a date with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a date with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you met her in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she said towards me, I have to go in a courtroom. We cannot go in the afternoon because the interpreter's sick and I have to translate. And another girl said, why don't you give him a pass to go in the-- there in that trial, because it is-- it is fascinating. So they-- yeah. We can do that. So they got me a pass to go in the courtroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once inside the courtroom, what did you hear or see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I hear the-- a fellow talking about the murders in Eastern Europe. And the guy is talking and talking and talking. And I say, hey. The guy is talking baloney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the-- a prosecutor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who it was. This is all wrong. And we-- he was talking to, I know the fellow that was Eduard Strauch. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a major or-- or-- or [GERMAN] in the SS. The butcher from Minsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while, the girl comes, and I tell her, listen. What's going on in the courtroom? Who is that joker that's the prosecutor? Why? So I tell her, he is wrong. So and so. The ghetto got liquidated then, then, and then, and this, this, and this. You sure? I says, look. I pick up the clothing when I worked in the motor pool. She said, wait a minute. She goes over and knocks on the wood there where the separation is. And calls the prosecutor over. And tells that to the prosecutor. And the prosecutor said, wait a minute. How do you know that? Well, a friend just told me. And he said, right. Was your friend in the SS? And she said, no. He is a survivor. He is Jewish. You want to talk to him? So you went to meet the prosecutor?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I meet the prosecutor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin Ferencz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you met the prosecutor, what did you tell him?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I knew about Jost. What I knew about Sandberger. And what I knew about Strauch. All of the mass killings, what I have witnesses and what I know about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, are you sure? I said, yes, I am sure. So and so and so. I worked in the motor pool for the Gestapo. In the [GERMAN]. So he said, can you stay here for a few days? We want to talk to you. I said, if I have to, yeah. Well, we can put you in a hotel if I have to. And-- and the girl said, no. You don't have to put him in a hotel. I can take care of him and make sure he stays here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start giving your deposition?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next-- the next day, we started already. But they-- it was not a deposition. Let's say for the next two weeks I had an interrogation going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: An interrogation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of the court did this take place in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in a-- in a separate rooms where the prosecutor were. Not in-- in the courtroom. What they wanted me to do, go in the courtroom, listen to it, and tell them where they makes mistakes or what I remember, and give them the information.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew three of them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the number and the appearance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- let's say three of them. Two of the defendants. One was Dr. iur. Eduard Strauch. The other one was Dr. iur. Sandberger. From Riga was Dr. iur. Rudolf Lange. They were lawyers. And the-- one of the defendant, Heinz Jost, I only have seen once. He was a general. He came to Salaspils when we make the mass grave. And he was the one when-- when the [GERMAN] was, where 5,000 Jews in Riga got killed in March 1942. He was in charge then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of security did you notice during Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were under quite a-- a guard, and you could not go in the courtroom without a pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet other members of the prosecution--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you list those members that you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lieutenant [? Schwartz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was in that same lawsuit. Then I met a fellow named Dodd. He was later a senator here from Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: The father.

speaker: interviewee
text: The father. And I broke his neck too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was involved in [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1964, they were closing the armory in Springfield--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was making weapons for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, let's-- let's stay with Nuremberg for right now. Can you describe other members of the prosecution counsel there--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was somebody else, but I don't know in which case he was involved. Clancy was his name. He was a lawyer later on in Springfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the number of the case that you gave the depositions for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Case Nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the case specifically about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ohlendorf. He admitted in the courtroom that 90,000 Jews got shot under his command. And when Michael Musmanno, the presiding judge, ask him, what would you have done if your mother or sister would have been one of the victims? He answered, if I had had the order, I would have shot them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Musmanno said, court martial, takes the witness back to the bench with the other defendants. I have heard enough of him. Can you describe what the defense counsel looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very aggressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very aggressive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because they first saw what freedom in the courtroom means, which was never in Germany before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other key witnesses or memorable testimony you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know. Not of one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the judges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Musmanno was very aggressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- here from the United States. And he was later on-- on the Pennsylvania Supreme Court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other judges that impressed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no contact with.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about court clerks? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody. No contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: No contact. And what about the media? Can you describe some of the media?

speaker: interviewee
text: The media was under control of the military government. So there is nothing to say about--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other class-- courtroom personnel that you noticed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no contact with, because I had a pass and I had to go in a special section.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel during this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the Germans should have been treated like we were treated in a concentration camp, and not like babies. They were treated like gentlemen in a courtroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to see that differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it didn't make sense to me. They were criminals, but they were not treated like criminals.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to stay and give your deposition?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- on and off altogether for three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for those three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out with a girl. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, describe this girl's job in court and what was her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a-- Greta [? Wolf. ?] She was an interpreter. And she was later on in charge of the typing pool. But most of her time, she was interpreter and-- when the German physicians were on trial because she was a registered nurse and midwife in England. So she knew all the medical terms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you dating during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to date towards this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you date for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married her in July 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year after you met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gothenburg, Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about the outcome of this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very good, but it made me sick that the Germans were let go after so few years in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the evidence affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did not affect me at all because-- it left me cold because I still had nightmares from it, and I still have on and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. How did the experience of the trial itself affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt that justice was to be done finally. And it should have been done in a more radical way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were not in court, what did you do with your time off in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to see the theater in the evening, or we went out taking trips around Nuremberg and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there special clubs and restaurants you liked? A certain--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing in Nuremberg I could do, except-- which was illegal for me, and I went anyway with her-- was the American restaurants and things like that. Which was illegal for German and-- and DPs. But I went with her anyway, because she was a member of the United States Forces at that case.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were prohibited--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from certain places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there security rules and regulations the whole time that you were in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends, besides your fiancee?

speaker: interviewee
text: German? None. I had no friends in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole time you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not feel at home there while I was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see romances develop between anyone in the trial community, besides yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience a romance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were involved with this romance during your entire stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
